text: A 63-year-old man is complaining of substernal chest pain radiating to his left arm and jaw. He rates his pain an 8 on a 1-10 scale. Baseline vital signs are: BP 136/70, P 126, R 16. The patient takes no medications regularly and has no allergies to medications. You should now:
options: Attach the external defibrillator pads to the patient's chest.
Contact medical control and request permission to administer nitroglycerin.
Ask your partner to assist the patient's respirations with a bag-valve-device.
Place the patient in a position of comfort and administer high-concentration oxygen by nonrebreather mask.

text: 63-year-old man is complaining of substernal chest pain radiating to his left arm and jaw. He rates his pain an 8 on a 1-10 scale. Baseline vital signs are: BP 136/70, P 126, R 16. The patient takes no medications regularly and has no allergies to medications. You should now :
options: Attach the external defibrillator pads to the patient's chest.
Contact medical control and request permission to administer nitroglycerin
Ask your partner to assist the patient's respirations with a bag-valve-device.
Place the patient in a position of comfort and administer high-concentration oxygen by nonrebreather mask.

text: A physician prescribed medications that patient already have and you may assist patients to administration with approval from medical direction are :
One factor which does not shown in ECG is:
hint: ECG is a snapshot of electrical activity, not history.
explanation: ECG shows rate, rhythm, and indirectly strength (amplitude), but not when the problem started.

text: You are evaluating an ECG of a patient, PR interval is more than 0.20sec, QRS regular. What should you suspect?
hint: Prolonged PR with regular rhythm is characteristic.
explanation: Prolonged PR interval (>0.20 sec) with 1:1 conduction = 1st-degree AV block.

text: Identify the ecg
explanation: Atrial flutter is characterized by sawtooth flutter waves, especially visible in leads II, III, aVF.

text: One likely cause of the bradycardia is:
explanation: Parasympathetic (vagal) stimulation slows heart rate. Fever, pain, and blood loss typically cause tachycardia.

text: One of the following is not from the medications that carried on the EMT unit.
The side effects of the prescribed inhalers are increased pulse rate, tremor, nervousness, nausea.

you dispatched to a patient 77 years old femal, in yours assessment you found here in acold clammy skin, seizure. increased heart rate, here relatives told you that she s took here insulin shot before breakfast, as an EMT-BASIC whats yours diagnose to this case
A medication used to dilate the bronchioles and causes the blood vessels to constrict is:
Which of the following is the drug's trade name:
Nitroglycerin may not be given if the patient :
options: Has used two tablets of nitroglycerin.
Has a systolic blood pressure less than 100.
Has a diastolic blood pressure less than 90.
Has taken one tablet with no

text: A myocardial infarction occurs when :
options: A heart valve does not close completely
A heart valve does not open fully
There is a blockage in a coronary artery
The electrical impulses of the heart become irregular

text: Your adult patient with chest pain has a blood pressure of 100/54,a pulse of 112 and has a history of angina. You should :
options: Administer the patient's prescribed nitroglycerin after consult medical direction.

text: Excitability and anxiety, increased heart rateand headache, consider Side effects Epinephrine:
You are examining a woman in her eighth month of pregnancy and discover that her blood pressure is 100/70, her heart rate is 90, and her respirations are 20. These vital signs indicate:
options: Moderate hypovolemia at this stage of pregnancy

text: On 1-minute evaluation of a newborn infant, you note the infant has blue arms and legs, a pink trunk, and a heart rate of 120. He is crying loudly and moving all extremities. The patient sneezes when stimulated. This patient's APGAR score is:
During the initial assessment, an irregular heart rate would most likely be discovered by:
Which of the following terms best describes "shock"?

You are dispatched to a call for a 36 year old male construction site worker who is complaining of anxiety like symptoms at work. Upon your arrival the worker tells you that he feels palpitations, chest discomfort, vomiting, nausea, sweating and shaking. 12 lead ECG reveals no changes in relation to myocardial infarction. Based on the presentation of symptoms what is the condition of the patient
Due to which of the following conditions are cardiac abnormalities or dysrhythmias common in patients with eating disorders
options: Bleeding Of Gl tract or esophagus

text: A 36-year-old male sustains blunt force thoracic trauma and fits the criteria for a load-and-go patient. Which of the following should be performed on the scene?
options: Obtain a finger-stick serum lactate level
Assess for other potentially life-threatening conditions
explanation: The purpose of the initial assessment is to prioritize the patient and to identify all immediately life-threatening conditions. The information gathered is used to make decisions about critical interventions and time of transport.

text: A pericardiocentesis is performed to:
options: To remove fluid from the lining around the lungs
To remove fluid from the lining around the heart
To inject medications directly into the heart
explanation: The pericardial sac is an inelastic membrane that surrounds the heart. If blood collects rapidly between the heart and pericardium from a cardiac injury, the ventricles of the heart will be compressed, making the heart less able to refill, and cardiac output falls.

text: Which finding requires interruption of the ITLS Primary Survey?
explanation: You may interrupt the assessment sequence only if (1) the scene becomes unsafe, (2) you must treat exsanguinating hemorrhage, (3) you must treat an airway obstruction, or (4) you must treat cardiac arrest. (Respiratory arrest, dyspnea, or bleeding management should be delegated to other team members while you continue assessment of the patient.)

text: Which one of the following is a reason to interrupt the initial assessment?
options: Severe head injury with brain tissue visible
explanation: Remember, once you begin patient assessment in the ITLS Primary Survey, only four things should cause you to interrupt completion of the assessment. You may interrupt the assessment sequence only if (1) the scene becomes unsafe, (2) you must treat exsanguinating hemorrhage, (3) you must treat an airway obstruction, or (4) you must treat cardiac arrest.

text: What is the most serious early complication of burns from electrical contact?
explanation: The most serious and immediate injury that results from electrical contact is cardiac dysrhythmia.

text: Which of the following distinguishes a tension pneumothorax from a simple pneumothorax?
explanation: Clinical signs of a tension pneumothorax include dyspnea, anxiety, tachypnea, distended neck veins, and possibly tracheal deviation away from the affected side. Auscultation will reveal diminished breath sounds on the affected side and will be accompanied by hyperresonance when percussed. Shock with hypotension will follow and is not present with a simple pneumothorax.

text: A 42-year-old male is involved in a motorcycle collision. Which of the following assessment findings should be managed first?
explanation: If your patient has major bleeding, the priority is C-A-B-C. The first C stands for control life-threatening bleeding. (Do not confuse this with the American Heart Association/ILCOR’s “CAB” for cardiac arrest, where the C stands for compressions.) If your patient has major external bleeding, you must immediately control it.

text: A 32-year-old male is involved in a motor vehicle collision. The steering wheel is bent. Your assessment reveals present and equal bilateral breath sounds and a rapid and weak radial pulse that disappears upon inspiration. You should suspect:
explanation: Pulsus paradoxus, or paradoxical pulse, may be noted. This is where the radial pulse is not felt with inspiration. The major differential diagnosis in the field is tension pneumothorax. With cardiac tamponade, the patient will be in shock with equal breath sounds and a midline.

text: What is the most common cause of cardiopulmonary arrest in the trauma patient?
explanation: Hypoxemia is the most common cause of traumatic cardiopulmonary arrest. Acute airway obstruction or ineffective breathing will be clinically manifested as hypoxemia.

text: A 24-year-old male is involved in a head-on collision. If you suspect:
options: Lower leg injuries, administer fluid resuscitation at 20 ml/kg
A head injury, administer an anti-seizure medication
A chest injury, obtain an EKG
explanation: Consider a bruised chest wall as a myocardial contusion that requires monitoring of cardiac rhythm and, if available, a 12-lead ECG.

text: A 54-year-old male is involved in a motor vehicle collision. The steering wheel is bent. During your initial assessment, you note his skin is pale, radial pulses are present and breath sounds are clear. Which one of the following is most consistent with these findings?
hint: Bent wheel + stable lungs ? cardiac contusion
explanation: Bruising of the heart is basically the same injury as an acute myocardial infarction.

text: A 23-year-old female fell from a second-floor balcony. Upon arrival, you find her lying in the grass. She responds to verbal commands and your assessment reveals flat neck veins, and normal chest, abdomen and pelvis examinations. Her skin is cool, clammy and ashen; respirations are rapid and shallow; radial pulses are too rapid to count and thready. You place her on the heart monitor and it shows a wide-complex tachycardia of about 280 per minute. You should suspect:
explanation: If cardiac output falls (either due to dropping or a very fast heart rate or lowered stroke volume), blood pressure will fall.

text: A 78-year-old female driver struck a parked car while she was traveling at a low rate of speed. She is complaining of shortness of breath and chest pain. Your assessment and treatment should include:
options: Breath sounds, history, place in a position of comfort
explanation: All elderly patients should have cardiac monitoring, pulse oximetry, and capnography, if available.

text: Which one of the following is typically associated with post-traumatic hemorrhage early shock?
options: Loss of 30% to 45% of blood volume
explanation: Early shock is the loss of approximately 15% to 25% of blood volume. That is enough to stimulate slight to moderate tachycardia, pallor, narrowed pulse pressure, thirst, weakness, and possibly delayed capillary refill.

text: Which of the following is not caused by the release of catecholamines during shock?
explanation: Catecholamines (epinephrine and norepinephrine) increase both the rate and strength of the heart's contractions, constrict peripheral arterial blood vessels and affects the sweat glands. Hypotension is the result of the inability to compensate.

text: A 49-year-old male was crossing the street and struck by a truck. First responders are doing CPR. The patient is pulseless, apneic and the monitor shows asystole. Your assessment reveals an open skull fracture and deformed pelvis. Based on the ITLS curriculum, which of the following is the most appropriate act?
options: Immediately intubate the patient to secure the airway
Establish intravenous access and administer a 1 liter bolus
Establish intravenous access and administer 2 mg of atropine
Resuscitative efforts should be discontinued, unless prohibited by law
explanation: Resuscitation should be withheld in cases of: (a) blunt trauma with no breathing, pulse, or organized rhythm on ECG on EMS arrival at the scene; (b) penetrating trauma with no breathing, pulse, pupillary reflexes, spontaneous movement, or organized ECG activity; (c) any trauma with injuries obviously incompatible with life (such as decapitation); (d) any trauma with evidence of significant time lapse since pulselessness, including dependent lividity, rigor mortis, etc.

text: A 23-year-old male fell from the roof of a building and reports that he cannot feel his arms or legs. You should first:
options: Apply a cervical collar and transport
Obtain a blood pressure and SAO2
explanation: Once you begin patient assessment in the ITLS Primary Survey, only four things should cause you to interrupt completion of the assessment. You may interrupt the assessment sequence only if (1) the scene becomes unsafe, (2) you must treat exsanguinating hemorrhage, (3) you must treat an airway obstruction, or (4) you must treat cardiac arrest.

text: A 16-year-old male sustained a gunshot wound to the left chest and complains of shortness of breath. He presents with rapid and shallow respirations, absent radial pulses, and cool, clammy, pale skin. His neck veins are distended and there is an absence of breath sounds over the left side. You should suspect:
explanation: Any traumatic or medical condition that slows or prevents the venous return of blood can cause shock by lowering cardiac output and thus oxygen delivery to the tissues. Likewise, anything that obstructs the flow of blood to or through the heart can cause shock.

text: A 4-month-old infant who has had vomiting and diarrhea for the past 36 hours is lethargic, has a weak cry, and has mottled skin color. Vital signs are BP 62/46, HR 172, and RR 48. What do the signs and symptoms suggest?
explanation: The history of significant fluid loss (vomiting/diarrhea), tachycardia, hypotension, and signs of poor perfusion (lethargy, mottling, weak cry) are classic for hypovolemic shock. Respiratory arrest would present with bradypnea or apnea; meningitis typically includes fever; cardiogenic shock usually arises from a known cardiac condition.

text: When is it appropriate to use an AED in a witnessed pediatric cardiac arrest?
options: Once the patient is in the ambulance.
When the ambulance is en route to the hospital.
explanation: Early defibrillation is a key link in the chain of survival. For a witnessed pediatric cardiac arrest, the AED should be applied and used as soon as it is available. Delaying defibrillation reduces the chance of successful resuscitation.

text: A 3-month-old infant is found unresponsive in his crib and has cool, mottled skin. His vital signs are HR 50 and RR 6. What is the initial treatment for this patient?
options: Establish IV access and administer an IV fluid bolus of 20 mL/kg.
Establish IV access and administer 0.01 mg/kg IV epinephrine 1:10,000.
Perform chest compressions and ventilate with a bag-mask device.
Initiate bag-mask ventilation and then reassess his skin color and heart rate.
explanation: An infant with a heart rate less than 60 bpm and signs of poor perfusion requires immediate cardiopulmonary resuscitation, starting with chest compressions and ventilations. Ventilation alone may be insufficient; bradycardia with poor perfusion is treated as a cardiac arrest.

text: What is the dysrhythmia for a 3-year-old with a heart rate of 180 beats/min, a narrow QRS complex, and a variable R-R interval called?
explanation: Sinus tachycardia in a 3-year-old has a heart rate usually less than 220 bpm, a narrow QRS, and often some beat-to-beat variability (sinus arrhythmia). SVT typically has a rate exceeding 220 bpm, is abrupt in onset, and has a fixed R-R interval. Ventricular tachycardia has a wide QRS.

text: A 12-year-old boy was shot in the abdomen during a hunting accident. He has altered mental status and his skin is cool and clammy. Vital signs are BP 64/46, weak HR 142, and RR 38. What do the signs and symptoms suggest?
explanation: Penetrating abdominal trauma with hypotension, tachycardia, and altered mental status indicates hypovolemic shock from internal hemorrhage. There are no signs of tension pneumothorax (tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds), neurogenic shock (spinal injury, bradycardia), or cardiac tamponade (muffled heart sounds, JVD).

text: A 2-year-old girl has been having respiratory distress for about an hour. She has crackles noted in her lower lobes and her lips are cyanotic. She is semi-responsive with pale and diaphoretic skin. It is difficult to obtain a BP. Other vital signs are HR 270 and RR 60. What type of shock is this patient experiencing?
explanation: The extreme tachycardia (HR 270) is most consistent with supraventricular tachycardia, not sinus tachycardia. Combined with crackles and respiratory distress, this suggests heart failure due to a primary arrhythmia, causing cardiogenic shock. No history of fluid loss, infection, or spinal trauma exists.

text: What is the dysrhythmia for a 16-year-old with a heart rate of 180 beats/min, a narrow QRS complex, and a nonvariable RR interval?
explanation: A rate of 180, narrow QRS, and a regular (nonvariable) R-R interval is classic for supraventricular tachycardia (SVT). Sinus tachycardia usually has some beat-to-beat variability and rates below 220 in adolescents. Ventricular tachycardia has a wide QRS.

text: Which of the following findings is the first to present when a child is in a state of compensated shock?
explanation: Tachycardia is the earliest and most consistent sign of compensated shock in children.

text: A child has a heart rate between 180 - 188 beats/min with a narrow QRS and a strong pulse. Which of the following best describes his rhythm?
explanation: In children, sinus tachycardia can reach rates of ~180–200 bpm. A rate of 180–188 bpm with a strong pulse may still represent sinus tachycardia unless it is very regular, abrupt in onset, and lacks variability, which would suggest SVT.

text: What is the dysrhythmia for a 16-year-old with a heart rate of 180 beats/min, a narrow QRS complex, and a non-variable RR interval?
explanation: A narrow-complex tachycardia with a regular rhythm and no variability suggests SVT. Sinus tachycardia typically shows variability and is associated with an underlying cause (e.g., fever, dehydration).

text: What is one of the 1st signs of compensated shock in children?
explanation: Tachycardia is one of the earliest signs of compensated shock in children.

text: A patient presents with a recent onset of shortness of breath with exertion. She is presently receiving chemotherapy for treatment of breast cancer. Your physical exam reveals jugular venous distention and muffled heart sounds. Of the following, the most likely etiology of these findings is:
explanation: Beck's triad (JVD, muffled heart tones, hypotension) is classic for cardiac tamponade. Chemotherapy (especially with certain agents) can cause pericardial effusion leading to tamponade. Pneumothorax would have absent breath sounds; pulmonary embolism presents with sudden dyspnea/pleuritic pain; pericarditis often has chest pain worse lying flat.

text: Which of the following symptoms is best matched to an assessment which would be helpful in determining a cause?
explanation: Headache requires a neurologic exam to rule out stroke or other neurological causes. Dyspnea ? pulse oximetry (not blood glucose); chest pain ? ECG (not orthostatics); abdominal pain ? physical exam (not pulse oximetry).

text: A nasopharyngeal airway is most indicated to a patient with (in):
options: Respiratory distress and wheezing in all lung fields
explanation: NPA is indicated when airway obstruction from tongue or soft tissue (snoring respirations) and patient has a gag reflex (not appropriate in arrest where OPA is preferred). Hypoglycemia with snoring respirations ? NPA helps maintain airway while treating glucose. In cardiac arrest, OPA is preferred. Wheezing and tachycardia don't necessarily indicate need for an airway adjunct.

text: Which of the following signs is most helpful in differentiating allergic reaction from anaphylaxis?
explanation: Hypotension indicates cardiovascular involvement (distributive shock) – the hallmark of anaphylaxis. Allergic reaction alone does not cause hypotension. Hives, itching, and tachycardia can occur in both, though tachycardia is more severe in anaphylaxis.

text: Which of the following occurs secondary to coronary artery occlusion?
explanation: Coronary artery occlusion leads to myocardial infarction (lack of blood flow to heart muscle causing necrosis). PE is from pulmonary artery; tamponade from pericardial effusion; dissection is from aortic wall tear.

text: Of the following signs and symptoms, which is the best indicator of inadequate perfusion?
explanation: Altered mental status is the most reliable indicator of inadequate perfusion (brain hypoperfusion). Pale skin, tachycardia, tachypnea are earlier compensated signs. When the brain is affected, compensation is failing.

text: A 52-year-old female presents confused but alert complaining of fatigue, tingling in her fingers bilaterally, and chest palpitations. She has a history of AMI, HTN, and renal failure and has missed her last two dialysis treatments. Vital signs are HR=102 and irregular, BP=142/90, RR=18. Of the following, which is the most likely?
hint: Missed dialysis + arrhythmia + tingling ? potassium issue.
explanation: Missed dialysis + tingling (paresthesia) + palpitations (irregular HR) = hyperkalemia. Hyperkalemia is common in patients who miss dialysis and can cause ECG changes (peaked T waves, wide QRS, sine wave). AMI less likely without chest pain; hypovolemia would have hypotension; hyperventilation unlikely to cause tingling bilaterally in this context.

text: Which of the following is most likely to present with hypertension and bradycardia?
explanation: Cushing's triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations) indicates increased intracranial pressure, often from intracranial hemorrhage. Hypoglycemia causes tachycardia; ischemia (MI) can cause bradycardia but not hypertension; migraine may have normal vitals.

text: A 71-year-old male is complaining of an acute onset of right sided weakness and aphasia. Of the following, which would be most helpful in ruling out a non-stroke etiology?
explanation: Hypoglycemia can mimic stroke symptoms (focal neurological deficits). Checking blood glucose quickly rules out hypoglycemia as a cause. ECG (A) rules out cardiac causes but not stroke mimics; BP (C) and SpO2 (D) are supportive but not definitive.

text: All the following are potential causes of syncope EXCEPT:
explanation: Fever alone does not cause syncope (passing out). Cardiac dysrhythmia, vasovagal, and hypovolemia are common causes of syncope. Fever may cause lightheadedness from dehydration but syncope is not a direct effect.

text: A 78-year-old female presents with a sudden onset of respiratory distress, which came on while she was at rest. She is anxious and agitated. Vital signs are BP = 78/36, pulse = 110, RR = 22. Abnormal findings include crackles bilaterally, cyanosis. Which of the following medications would be most effective?
explanation: Severe hypotension with respiratory distress and bilateral crackles suggests cardiogenic shock with pulmonary edema and poor perfusion. Inotropic vasopressors (e.g., dopamine) are required to support cardiac output and blood pressure. Diuretics may worsen hypotension in this unstable patient.

text: You are assessing a 69-year-old male, complaining of chest discomfort. His 12-Lead ECG reveals ST depression in leads V1-V4. There is no ST elevation in any lead. This ECG finding is most consistent with:
hint: ST depression V1–V4 ? posterior MI clue.
explanation: ST depression in V1-V4 (anterior leads) with tall R waves suggests posterior MI. Posterior MI is diagnosed by reciprocal changes (ST depression in anterior leads) or posterior leads V7-V9. Inferior MI has ST elevation in II, III, aVF; anterior MI has ST elevation in V1-V4; pericarditis has diffuse ST elevation.

text: Symptomatic hyperkalemia should be treated with which of the following medication combinations?
options: Sodium bicarbonate, naloxone, and calcium chloride
explanation: Management of symptomatic hyperkalemia includes calcium (to stabilize cardiac membranes), agents that shift potassium intracellularly (e.g., insulin with glucose, albuterol), and agents that remove potassium (e.g., furosemide). Although option D is incomplete without insulin and glucose, it is the most appropriate combination among the choices provided.

text: A 34-year-old female presents with dyspnea, audible wheezing and responds to painful stimulus. Her husband states that she used her inhaler multiple times without relief. Of the following, the best treatment would be?
hint: Severe asthma + low LOC ? ventilate first.
explanation: The patient has life-threatening asthma with decreased level of consciousness (responds only to painful stimulus), indicating impending respiratory failure. Immediate priority is airway and ventilation support with a BVM. Nebulized beta-2 agonists (e.g., salbutamol) are first-line in severe asthma, but require adequate respiratory effort and airway protection, which this patient lacks. IM epinephrine can be considered in severe or refractory asthma, but it does not replace the need for immediate ventilatory support in a patient with altered mental status. The listed dose is also incorrect (1 mg 1:10,000 is for IV use in cardiac arrest, not IM). Therefore, assisted ventilation is the most appropriate immediate intervention.

text: A 62-year-old female presents with an acute onset of weakness and dizziness. Vital signs are HR = 52, BP = 240/160, RR = 18. Which of the following actions is the most appropriate?
hint: HTN + brady + neuro symptoms ? stroke screen.
explanation: Severe hypertension with weakness/dizziness + bradycardia ? possible intracranial hemorrhage or hypertensive emergency. Perform stroke assessment first to rule out stroke before giving blood pressure meds or cardiac interventions. Nitroglycerin could lower BP too rapidly in stroke; atropine for bradycardia is not first; pacing not indicated.

text: A patient presents hemodynamically stable complaining of chest discomfort. A 12-Lead ECG reveals ST segment elevation in all the precordial leads as well as II, III, and aVF. Which of the following is the most likely etiology of these ECG findings?
explanation: Widespread ST elevation in multiple leads (anterior + inferior) suggests pericarditis (diffuse inflammation). Acute MI typically localizes to a territory (e.g., inferior leads II, III, aVF or anterior V1-V4). Aortic dissection and PE do not cause diffuse ST elevation.

text: A 52-year-old male presents complaining of chest discomfort. He is alert and oriented with a blood pressure of 110/70. You obtain the 12-Lead ECG, Shows an Inferior Wall MI (ST elevation in II, III, aVF). Of the following, the most appropriate next action would be to:
hint: Inferior MI ? check right-sided ECG before nitrates.
explanation: The 12-lead ECG showed an Inferior Wall MI (ST elevation in II, III, aVF), the immediate next step is to obtain a right-sided ECG (V4R). This is critical to rule out right ventricular infarction before administering nitroglycerin, as NTG reduces preload and can cause profound, life-threatening hypotension in patients with RV infarcts.

text: Which of the following medications is intended to decrease myocardial workload and increase myocardial blood flow?
You are called for a 6-month-old infant with respiratory distress. Which of the following is true about patients in this age group?
options: infants require forceful ventilations with the BVM
infants are more resistant to hypoxia than adults
infants rarely develop bradycardia due to hypoxia

text: You are treating a patient with respiratory distress. He has a history of multiple myocardial infarctions. Lung sounds indicate pulmonary edema. You note the patient does NOT have any JVD or pedal edema. The patient is most likely experiencing:
Which of the following describes the purpose of administering aspirin to a suspected myocardial infarction patient?
options: Aspirin will eliminate cardiac related chest pain
Aspirin reduces platelet aggregation in the coronary arteries
Aspirin reduces the risk of metabolic acidosis

text: You are treating an unresponsive patient with slow, shallow respirations and pinpoint pupils. Which of the following conditions is most likely?
Which of the following signs of hypoxia is more common in pediatric patients?
Which of the following medications is intended to decrease myocardial workload and increase myocardial blood flow?
You are called for a 6-month-old infant with respiratory distress. Which of the following is true about patients in this age group?
options: infants require forceful ventilations with the BVM
infants are more resistant to hypoxia than adults
infants rarely develop bradycardia due to hypoxia

text: You are treating a patient with respiratory distress. He has a history of multiple myocardial infarctions. Lung sounds indicate pulmonary edema. You note the patient does NOT have any JVD or pedal edema. The patient is most likely experiencing:
Which of the following describes the purpose of administering aspirin to a suspected myocardial infarction patient?
options: Aspirin will eliminate cardiac related chest pain
Aspirin reduces platelet aggregation in the coronary arteries
Aspirin reduces the risk of metabolic acidosis

text: You are treating an unresponsive patient with slow, shallow respirations and pinpoint pupils. Which of the following conditions is most likely?
Which of the following signs of hypoxia is more common in pediatric patients?
